name: Interleukin 33
summary: IL33 encodes interleukin-33, an epithelial-derived cytokine often described as an 'alarmin' that can be released during barrier stress or injury. IL-33 can promote type 2 immune responses via signaling through the IL1RL1 (ST2) receptor on immune cells such as ILC2s and mast cells. Population genetic studies repeatedly implicate the IL33/IL1RL1 region in asthma and allergic phenotypes, motivating mechanistic hypotheses that connect airway exposures to epithelial stress signaling and downstream inflammation.

regulatory_notes.promoter_activity: Regulatory variation near IL33 is associated with allergic disease signals in multiple GWAS.
regulatory_notes.enhancer_associations: Enhancer context is tissue-specific; seed content summarizes evidence qualitatively.
regulatory_notes.methylation_sensitivity: Epigenetic regulation is plausible in exposure contexts, but seed evidence is summarized conservatively.

mechanistic_hypotheses.hypothesis: Airway particulate exposure increases epithelial stress signaling that promotes IL-33 release, priming type 2 inflammation in asthma-relevant tissues.
mechanistic_hypotheses.supporting_evidence: Supported by exposure biology reviews and seed mechanistic brief evidence synthesis; not interpreted as individual risk prediction.

references.title: A large-scale, consortium-based genome-wide association study meta-analysis of asthma
The GTEx Consortium atlas of genetic regulatory effects across human tissues (v8)
IL-33 and the biology of the epithelial barrier in allergic inflammation
